posts.name: Alan Eden
posts.cooked:
Have you ever opened a prized bottle just for the sheer fun of it without planning on a Tuesday night with a Big Mac ?

Day and food variable but you get the idea, just spur of the moment opening something ridiculously expensive and/or rare for no reason other than the whim took you and you said f*ck it why not

posts.name: Ron Slye
posts.cooked:
Slightly different than what you ask, but I sometimes have a single old bottle, that may or may not be good. If it is good, I wish that I could share with others who will appreciate it. If not, then I would rather have it alone.

So the other night I opened my last bottle of 1978 Talbot. The bottle had gone through some serious humidity in its prior life (while in my care), and the label was not only all scrunched up, but it was mostly on the back side of the bottle. Cork was perfect. And the wine was sublime. Luckily both my wife loves wine (so she was able to enjoy it), and a rep from a local winery stopped by to drop off some wine we had bought, so she got to have a taste as well.

I do think I have done something more directly related to the OP, but I can’t remember the specifics!

posts.name: Marcus Dean
posts.cooked: 85 Cheval Blanc about 5 months ago.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:
Open that bottle Night is coming this month.

I consider February to be open that bottle month.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: 1966 Quinta do Noval Nacional. Thought it was the regular Quinta do Noval. The greatness of the wine diminished the pain, but it was a pity not to plan an evening around it.

posts.name: Eric Egan
posts.cooked: Haut Brion '66 about a year ago when a couple my girlfriend knows came over and the discussion turned to wine. It transpired that they didn’t understand what the fuss was all about. They got it.

posts.name: J a y H a c k
posts.cooked:
Eric Egan:

Haut Brion '66 about a year ago when a couple my girlfriend knows came over and the discussion turned to wine. It transpired that they didn’t understand what the fuss was all about. They got it.

1976 Chateau de la Maltroye Clos de la Maltroye Chassagne Montrachet Rouge (in 2005-ish). Same idea. Having dinner with friends. One guys asks, “If you store wine correctly, will it last forever?” Rather than get into a big intellectual discussion, I went downstairs and grabbed this, which I had owned since release.

posts.cooked: 1996 Dalla Valle cab.

posts.name: Jim Freeman
posts.cooked: 1989 La Conseillante, or however it is spelled

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: done it often. Have often espoused on this board to make the wine the event, not wait for “a special day.” Have opened La Tache with a (mild) chili (and caught endless shit for it here), Leroy Musigny, many other special wines just for the heck of it.

posts.name: Dale Bowers
posts.cooked:
alan weinberg:

done it often. Have often espoused on this board to make the wine the event, not wait for “a special day.” Have opened La Tache with a (mild) chili (and caught endless shit for it here), Leroy Musigny, many other special wines just for the heck of it.

+100 Every time I open a wine it’s because i want to. Doesn’t matter the occasion or lack thereof.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: In around 1990 or so on the 4th of July we had another couple for dinner. I decided to try a wine I had purchased from a Burgundy producer I had never heard of before. David Schildknecht (who was in retail in DC at the time) had raved about the producer and the Chambolle Musigny les Sentiers 1985was very well priced. So, I had taken a chance on it. Opened the bottle, very pale in color. Wondered, David, what did you sell me? But then smelled it. Had the most wonderful nose. And a great palate to match. And, so that started me (and the friend I had over) on a life-long love affair with the wines of Jacky Truchot.

posts.name: Chris Sargent
posts.cooked: I’ve done this with bottles I thought would be great, with disappointing results (2009 Pontet Canet I’m looking at you! - yes I know, early, and it was ever-so slightly leaking at the cork which is why I opened it, which could account for it not showing well… I digress)… but recently I popped a 2001 Pavie because I just wanted something that I knew would kick ass, and I didn’t care much that I wasn’t having steak, or beef stew, or lamb… And it was great.

posts.name: Eric Ifune
posts.cooked: 1912 D’Oliveiras Verdelho just because I wanted something good. Runner up is a Quinta do Mourao San Leonardo 60 year old.
I have a 1964 Talbot standing up that I will probably open tomorrow night

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: I think it comes with the territory. If I have wine friends over, there is usually a moment when I go down to the cellar to pull one more bottle. Recently it was a 1970 Giscours, a magnificent Tremblay 2008 Chapelle Chambertin an Henri Bonneau Celestins.the wine is always served blind and I usually make the decision when I go down so it’s a tricky decant as the bottle has not been standing upright
